Home»Sport»Paris Olympics 2024»Paris 2024 Olympics: Team SA in TWO 200m semi-finals, here’s when

This 200m event should be tasty.

Team SA takes part in two of this year’s 200m semi-finals at the Paris 2024 Olympics. Here are all the details you need.

The men’s 200m semi-finals are run on Wednesday 07 August.The first semi at 20:02features, among others, Shaun Maswanganyi, Uganda’s Torsis Orogot, Canada’s Andre De Grasse and the dangerous young Jamaican Bryan Levell.Semi-final two at 20:10will be headlined by newly-crowned 100m champion Noah Lyles, who says this is his favourite event and Botswana star Letsile Tebogo. Wayde van Niekerk might fancy his chances in awide-open-looking third semifinal at 20:18.Half of this semi-final lineup hails from the African continent. Alongside van Niekerk are Nigeria’s Udodi Onwuzurike, Liberian athlete Joseph Fahnbulleh and Tapiwanashe Makarawu from Zimbabwe. 20-year-old South African Benjamin Richardson has withdrawn from the event, casting doubts over his fitness ahead of the 4×100 relays later in the games.

27-year-old American Noah Lyles beat Jamaican star Kishane Thompson by five-thousandths of a second to claim gold in a stunning time of 9.79 in one of the greatest finals in Olympic history. Fred Kerley, silver medallist in Tokyo, claimed the bronze. Akani Simbine heartbreakingly finished fourth, as he did in Tokyo, missing out on a medal by one-hundredth of a second. He beat Tokyo’s bronze medallist and Tokyo’s gold medallist Lamont Marcell Jacobs, but it wasn’t enough to get a podium place in Paris. All eight finalists finished within 0.12 seconds of the gold. Last-placed Jamaican Oblique Seville crossed the line in 9.91, which would have claimed fourth in Tokyo three years ago. It’s the first time in history that all men in the final crossed the line in under 10 seconds in a wind-legal race. And it meant, for the first time, that eight men had run under 10 seconds in a wind-legal race – making it the fastest race in history. It’s a familiar feeling for Simbine with consecutive fourth-placed finishes in Olympic finals. He finished fifth in Rio in 2016.

Novak Djokovic might have had some emotional scar tissue from his recent defeat to Carlos Alcaraz in the Wimbledon final in July. That wasn’t the case, however, as the37-year-old Serbian looked like a man possessedand simply unwilling to be denied. Djokovic beat Alcaraz in two pulsating tiebreaker sets of supreme quality on Court Philippe Chatrier in Paris to claim an elusive gold medal for his beloved Serbia. His only other medal at the Olympics came in 2008 when he claimed bronze as a 21-year-old. The long wait looked to be worth it.
